title: Jobs & Queues
scan: What you will learn: The Producer/Consumer pattern in practice — when to use a queue instead of a direct call, how jobs are structured, and why background processing is essential for resilient UIs
Why it matters: Any operation that takes more than 200ms and does not need to block the UI is a queue candidate — teams that skip queues build brittle synchronous chains that break under load
Queues decouple rate — the producer fires at user speed, the consumer fires at infrastructure speed — the correct model for bulk imports, email sends, and report generation
A job without a retry strategy is a fire-and-forget operation dressed up as reliable processing

blocks.html: ome work shouldn't make the user wait — sending mail, resizing an image, syncing a third party. Instead of doing it inline, you enqueue a job and let a worker drain the queue on its own time. Producer / Consumer, in practice.

blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    A[User Action] --> B[Producer]
    B --> C[(Queue)]
    C --> D[Consumer]
    D --> E[Result]
    D --fail--> C
    linkStyle 4 stroke:#e05252,stroke-width:2px
    C:::queueStyle
    classDef queueStyle fill:#f5a623,color:#000

blocks.html: Queues matter for campaign sends. A bulk email send to 500,000 recipients cannot run synchronously in a web request. It would time out in 30 seconds and leave no trace of how far it progressed. Queue-based architecture makes cancellation possible, progress visible, retry automatic, and failures auditable.

Infinite retry without backoff. A failed job that retries immediately at full rate hammers the downstream service, often triggering rate limiting or a cascade failure that takes the whole queue down. Implement exponential backoff with jitter as the default — not as a later optimisation.

items: Async — the request returns now; the work finishes later.
Retries — a failed job goes back on the queue instead of vanishing.
Throttling & order — control how fast and in what sequence work runs.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Jobs & Queues
items: Which work is async vs sync — and on what basis?
What is the retry policy per job type?
How are job failures made visible (logs, alerts, dashboards)?
Are queues bounded, and what happens at the bound?
Who is paged when the queue grows?
